title: Concurrency Scaling Guide
summary: Understand worker count, parallel fraction, coordination overhead, and why adding concurrency does not always create linear throughput.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates how much throughput may improve when workers or concurrency increase, while accounting for the portion of work that cannot scale cleanly.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch performance pressure before it becomes production trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when deciding whether to add workers, threads, processes, replicas, or service instances to improve throughput.
bullets: Early performance planning.
Expansion or load-growth review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining performance tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical performance planning terms.

items.label: Baseline Throughput (req/sec)
items.description: The current measured or expected throughput before adding more workers.
items.whyItMatters: The baseline anchors the scaling estimate. Without a realistic starting point, projected gains will be misleading.
items.commonMistake: Using a short synthetic benchmark instead of sustained throughput.

links.label: Compare baseline to throughput target
links.note: Use this when scaling is meant to reach a target load.

items.label: Workers (current)
items.description: The number of workers, threads, replicas, or processing lanes currently available.
items.whyItMatters: Current worker count shows the starting concurrency level and how much change the target represents.
items.commonMistake: Counting configured workers that are idle, blocked, or limited elsewhere.

links.label: Relate workers to queue capacity
links.note: Workers reduce queues only when they add real service capacity.

items.label: Workers (target)
items.description: The worker count being considered for the scaled design.
items.whyItMatters: Target worker count drives the projected gain and the risk of added coordination overhead.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every added worker contributes the same as the first worker.

links.label: Check CPU impact after scaling
links.note: More workers can shift the bottleneck to CPU utilization.

items.label: Parallel Fraction (%)
items.description: The percentage of the workload that can actually run in parallel.
items.whyItMatters: Parallel fraction controls how close scaling can get to linear improvement. Serial work limits the return from extra workers.
items.commonMistake: Assuming the whole workload is parallel because the application is multi-threaded.

items.label: Coordination Overhead (%)
items.description: The performance cost from locking, synchronization, messaging, scheduling, or shared-resource contention.
items.whyItMatters: Overhead reduces the useful gain from adding concurrency and often grows as worker count increases.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring coordination cost until scaling tests fail.

links.label: Use bottleneck analysis for shared-resource pressure
links.note: Coordination overhead often shows up as CPU, memory, disk, or network pressure.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means additional concurrency is likely to create useful throughput gain. Watch means scaling benefit is limited by serial work or coordination overhead. Risk means adding workers may produce poor return or amplify contention.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the architecture, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most performance planning mistakes come from looking at one symptom in isolation instead of tracing how latency, throughput, queues, concurrency, and resource pressure interact.
bullets: Sizing for average behavior while ignoring peak periods, bursts, retries, and background work.
Tuning one layer without checking whether the bottleneck simply moves to another resource.
Treating theoretical maximums as safe sustained production capacity.
Failing to document the assumptions behind latency targets, utilization limits, and growth margin.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace application testing, manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Measurements from the real platform are better than guesses whenever they are available.
Application behavior, storage design, network path, cache behavior, and workload mix can change the result.
Healthy results still need monitoring and validation after deployment.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, explain, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, production testing, vendor validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, software, platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, operating requirements, and SLA language.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or workload behavior changes.
